title: Jesus Is Condemned to Death
med: Pilate, knowing Jesus to be innocent, washes his hands and delivers Him to be crucified. The crowd cries out: "His blood be upon us and upon our children." Consider how Our Lord, who could have summoned twelve legions of angels, stands silent before His accusers. He accepts an unjust sentence so that we, who are justly condemned by our sins, might be set free. How often have I, like Pilate, known the right thing to do and failed to do it? How often have I condemned the innocent by my gossip, my rash judgments, my silence when I should have spoken? Lord, forgive my cowardice. Give me the courage to stand for truth even when it costs me everything.
stabat_lat:
Stabat Mater dolorósa,
iuxta Crucem lacrimósa,
dum pendébat Fílius.
stabat_eng:
At the Cross her station keeping,
stood the mournful Mother weeping,
close to Jesus to the last.

title: Jesus Carries His Cross
med: The heavy cross is laid upon the torn shoulders of Jesus. He embraces it willingly, for He sees in it the instrument of our salvation. Every splinter, every rough edge, is a sin of ours that He carries. The wood that was cursed in Eden becomes, through His love, the tree of life. Consider your own cross: the difficulties, the sufferings, the duties of your state in life that weigh upon you. Christ does not ask you to carry them alone. He carried yours first. Take up your cross daily, not with bitterness but with love, knowing that united to His, your suffering has infinite value.
stabat_lat:
Cuius ánimam geméntem,
contristátam et doléntem,
pertransívit gládius.
stabat_eng:
Through her heart, His sorrow sharing,
all His bitter anguish bearing,
now at length the sword had pass'd.

title: Jesus Falls the First Time
med: Weakened by the scourging, the crowning with thorns, and the weight of the cross, Jesus falls to the ground for the first time. The soldiers kick Him and drag Him upward. He rises. Consider that this fall represents our falls into venial sin: those daily failures, those small infidelities, those moments when we choose comfort over virtue. Yet He rises. And so must we. Every time we fall in our spiritual life, we must rise immediately through an act of contrition, never remaining on the ground in discouragement. The saints were not those who never fell, but those who always got up.
stabat_lat:
O quam tristis et afflícta
fuit illa benedícta
Mater Unigéniti!
stabat_eng:
Oh, how sad and sore distress'd
was that Mother highly blest
of the sole-begotten One!

title: Jesus Meets His Blessed Mother
med: In the midst of the jeering crowd, the eyes of Jesus meet the eyes of His Mother. No words pass between them, yet in that glance is contained all the sorrow of the world and all the love of Heaven. Mary's heart is pierced by the sword Simeon foretold. She cannot take His suffering away; she can only share it. Consider your own mother, who has suffered for you in ways you may never know. Consider Our Lady, who stands by every soul in its agony. When your suffering seems unbearable, look to Mary. She will not remove your cross, but she will stand beside you as you carry it.
stabat_lat:
Quæ mœrébat et dolébat,
pia Mater, dum vidébat
nati pœnas ínclyti.
stabat_eng:
Christ above in torment hangs;
she beneath beholds the pangs
of her dying glorious Son.

title: Simon Helps Carry the Cross
med: Simon of Cyrene, a man from the country, is compelled by the soldiers to help carry the cross. At first he resists; he did not choose this. But as he walks beside Jesus, something changes. The reluctant servant becomes a willing disciple. His sons Alexander and Rufus will later be known to the early Church. Consider the crosses that come to you uninvited: the illness you did not expect, the burden a neighbour places on you, the inconvenient demand of charity. What begins as compulsion can become the road to holiness, if you carry it with Christ.
stabat_lat:
Quis est homo qui non fleret,
Matrem Christi si vidéret
in tanto supplício?
stabat_eng:
Is there one who would not weep,
whelm'd in miseries so deep,
Christ's dear Mother to behold?

title: Veronica Wipes the Face of Jesus
med: A woman breaks through the line of soldiers, defying the guards, and presses a cloth to the bloodied face of Jesus. Tradition names her Veronica, from vera icon: "true image." On the cloth, the face of Christ is miraculously imprinted. Consider her courage: she risked punishment to perform a simple act of compassion. We encounter the suffering face of Christ in every person who is poor, sick, lonely, or despised. Do we have the courage of Veronica to break through our comfort and reach out? Every act of mercy, however small, receives the imprint of Christ's face upon our souls.
stabat_lat:
Quis non posset contristári
Christi Matrem contemplári
doléntem cum Fílio?
stabat_eng:
Can the human heart refrain
from partaking in her pain,
in that Mother's pain untold?

title: Jesus Falls a Second Time
med: Jesus falls a second time. The weight is heavier now, the body weaker. This fall represents our relapses into habitual sin: those patterns we thought we had overcome, those temptations we keep surrendering to, those confessions we keep repeating. The shame of falling again is itself a kind of suffering. But notice: Jesus does not stay down. He rises again, not because the cross is lighter, but because His love is stronger than His pain. Do not be discouraged by repeated failures. Go to Confession again. Rise again. The only true failure is to stop getting up.
stabat_lat:
Pro peccátis suæ gentis
vidit Iesum in torméntis,
et flagéllis súbditum.
stabat_eng:
Bruised, derided, curs'd, defiled,
she beheld her tender Child,
all with bloody scourges rent.

title: Jesus Meets the Women of Jerusalem
med: Jesus turns to the weeping women and says: "Daughters of Jerusalem, weep not for Me, but for yourselves and for your children." Even in His agony, He thinks of others. He weeps not for His own suffering but for the sins that cause suffering. Consider the tears you shed: are they for your wounded pride, your lost comforts, your frustrated desires? Or do you weep for your sins, which wound God and harm your soul? True repentance is not self-pity but sorrow born of love. Ask for the gift of tears: the grace to weep for what truly matters.
stabat_lat:
Vidit suum dulcem natum
moriéntem, desolátum,
dum emísit spíritum.
stabat_eng:
For the sins of His own nation,
she saw Jesus wracked with torments,
all with scourges rent.

title: Jesus Falls a Third Time
med: Jesus falls a third time, crushed beneath the weight of every sin ever committed and ever to be committed. This fall represents our most grievous sins: those mortal sins by which we turn completely away from God, those betrayals of grace that crucify Christ anew. He falls, and it seems impossible that He can rise. Yet He does. There is no sin so great that His mercy cannot reach it, no fall so deep that His hand cannot lift you. "Where sin abounded, grace did more abound" (Rom 5:20). If you have fallen into grave sin, do not despair. Rise. Go to Confession. He is waiting.
stabat_lat:
Eia, Mater, fons amóris,
me sentíre vim dolóris
fac, ut tecum lúgeam.
stabat_eng:
O thou Mother, fount of love,
touch my spirit from above,
make my heart with thine accord.

title: Jesus Is Stripped of His Garments
med: The soldiers strip Jesus of His garments, which have adhered to His wounds. The tearing of cloth from flesh reopens every wound. He stands exposed before the mocking crowd. Consider how sin strips us of the garment of grace, leaving us naked and ashamed before God, as Adam and Eve were naked in the garden. Consider also the virtue of modesty, which guards the dignity of the body as a temple of the Holy Spirit. Lord, clothe me again in sanctifying grace. Restore to me the robe of innocence lost by sin.
stabat_lat:
Fac ut árdeat cor meum
in amándo Christum Deum,
ut sibi compláceam.
stabat_eng:
Make me feel as thou hast felt;
make my soul to glow and melt
with the love of Christ my Lord.

title: Jesus Is Nailed to the Cross
med: The nails are driven through His hands and feet. The sound of the hammer echoes across Calvary. Each blow is a sin; each nail is driven by our choosing self over God. "They have pierced My hands and My feet; I can count all My bones" (Ps 21:17-18). Yet from those wounded hands flows the grace of every sacrament. The hands that blessed children, healed lepers, and broke bread are now fixed open in an eternal gesture of giving. What am I withholding from God that He asks of me? What am I clinging to that these nails would pry loose?
stabat_lat:
Sancta Mater, istud agas,
Crucifíxi fige plagas
cordi meo válide.
stabat_eng:
Holy Mother, pierce me through;
in my heart each wound renew
of my Saviour crucified.

title: Jesus Dies on the Cross
med: At the sixth hour, darkness covers the land. Jesus cries out: "My God, My God, why hast Thou forsaken Me?" Then: "It is finished." He bows His head and gives up His spirit. The veil of the Temple is torn from top to bottom. The earth quakes. The centurion declares: "Truly this was the Son of God." In this moment, death itself is conquered by the Author of life who enters it willingly. Every death of a Christian is now a birth. Every ending is a beginning. Stand at the foot of the Cross with Mary and John. Be silent. Adore.
stabat_lat:
Tui Nati vulneráti,
tam dignáti pro me pati,
pœnas mecum dívide.
stabat_eng:
Let me share with thee His pain,
who for all my sins was slain,
who for me in torments died.

title: Jesus Is Taken Down from the Cross
med: The body of Jesus is taken down from the Cross and placed in the arms of His Mother. The Pietà: the Mother holds her dead Son, as she once held Him as an infant in Bethlehem. The same hands that swaddled Him now receive His broken body. All seems lost. The disciples have fled. The mission appears to have failed. Yet Mary believes. In the darkest moment, she holds fast to faith. When everything in your life seems to have fallen apart, when God seems absent and prayer feels empty, remember Mary holding the dead Christ. She believed in the Resurrection before she saw it.
stabat_lat:
Fac me vere tecum flere,
Crucifíxo condolére,
donec ego víxero.
stabat_eng:
Let me mingle tears with thee,
mourning Him who mourn'd for me,
all the days that I may live.

title: Jesus Is Laid in the Tomb
med: The body of Jesus is anointed with spices and wrapped in a clean linen cloth. Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus, who were secret disciples, now come forward publicly. They lay Him in a new tomb cut from rock, and a great stone is rolled across the entrance. Silence. Darkness. The Sabbath rest begins. But this is not the end. The seed must fall into the ground and die before it bears fruit. The tomb is not a prison but a womb. In three days, He will shatter death from within. Every Good Friday leads to Easter Sunday. Wait in hope.
stabat_lat:
Quando corpus moriétur,
fac ut ánimæ donétur
paradísi glória. Amen.
stabat_eng:
While my body here decays,
may my soul Thy goodness praise,
safe in paradise with Thee. Amen.
